headline: A footrace at Olympia gives the ancient world its first shared calendar
summary: By later Greek reckoning, 776 BCE was the year of the first recorded Olympiad, when a cook from Elis named Coroebus won the two-hundred-yard stade race at a sanctuary of Zeus in the western Peloponnese. Historians from Diodorus Siculus to modern classicists have long used the date as a convenient hinge between the murky Greek Dark Age and the emerging archaic world of city-states, alphabetic writing, and Homeric epic. Around the same time, Neo-Assyrian kings were campaigning across the Zagros, Kushite chieftains were consolidating power at Napata on the Nile, Western Zhou China was drifting toward the crisis that would break it a generation later, and Villanovan smiths in Italy were laying the foundations of Etruscan urban life. Almost none of these societies had any idea the others existed.

snapshots.text: By tradition preserved by the fourth-century historian Diodorus Siculus and by the Elean chronicler Hippias, the first recorded Olympiad was celebrated in this year at Olympia, a sanctuary of Zeus in the western Peloponnese under the control of the small polis of Elis. The single event was the stade, a straight sprint of roughly one hundred and ninety-two metres, and the victor was Coroebus (Koroibos) of Elis, a cook by profession. After the race the presiding officials sacrificed a hecatomb of one hundred oxen on the great ash altar of Zeus. The date is a scholarly convention as much as a hard fact — the games almost certainly had earlier informal origins — but from this Olympiad onward Greek writers used the four-yearly cycle as a common chronological grid. Beyond Elis, mainland Greece around this time was a patchwork of emerging city-states experimenting with hoplite warfare, colonial ventures toward Sicily and the Black Sea, and, crucially, a new alphabet borrowed from Phoenician traders in the Levant. Homer's Iliad and Odyssey took roughly their canonical shape in these same decades. In central Italy, Villanovan iron-age communities in Etruria and Latium were coalescing into the settlements that would become Tarquinia, Veii, and Rome itself, traditionally founded around 753 BCE.

The Neo-Assyrian empire in these years was in a period of relative retrenchment under King Shalmaneser IV, who reigned from 782 to 773 BCE and would in 774 campaign against Damascus and toward Urartu on his northern frontier. Assyrian royal power was constrained by the influence of powerful magnates such as the general Shamshi-ilu, and eponym lists record plague and internal unrest through much of the decade. To the north, the kingdom of Urartu around Lake Van under King Argishti I was at its territorial and cultural peak, building fortress-cities like Erebuni (modern Yerevan, founded 782 BCE) and rivaling Assyria for control of the Armenian highlands. In the southern Levant, the parallel kingdoms of Israel under Jeroboam II and Judah under Uzziah were enjoying an unusual moment of prosperity and expansion described in the Hebrew Bible. Egypt was fractured among competing Libyan-descended dynasties: pharaohs of the Twenty-Second Dynasty at Tanis in the Delta, a rival Twenty-Third at Leontopolis, and local rulers such as the Chief of the Ma at Sais. Upstream at Napata, on the Nile's Fourth Cataract, the Kushite king Kashta was consolidating a Nubian state that within a generation would send its armies north under Piye to unify all Egypt under the Twenty-Fifth Dynasty.

The Western Zhou dynasty of China was in its final decades. The reigning king, traditionally King Xuan of Zhou (r. c. 827-782 BCE) or his troubled son King You (r. c. 781-771 BCE) depending on the chronology, ruled from the twin capitals of Haojing (near modern Xi'an) and Chengzhou (near Luoyang). The Zhou realm was a loose feudal patchwork of hereditary lordships bound to the king by ritual, kinship, and tribute, but by this decade the system was visibly weakening: earlier campaigns against the Xianyun and Rong tribes on the western marches had drained the treasury, and rival aristocratic lineages were asserting themselves. Within a generation, in 771 BCE, Quanrong nomads allied with disaffected Zhou vassals would sack Haojing and kill King You, forcing his heir to flee east to Chengzhou and inaugurating the long Eastern Zhou period and the Spring and Autumn era of contending states. In this same era, the great bronze-casting tradition of the Western Zhou, whose inscribed ritual vessels are among the earliest sustained Chinese written records, was reaching its aesthetic zenith. Further south, Bronze Age chiefdoms of the Yangtze and cultures such as the Sanxingdui-derived successors in Sichuan continued along independent trajectories, and on the Korean peninsula and Japanese archipelago, late Mumun and Late Jōmon societies practiced settled agriculture and pottery without state formation.

The Indian subcontinent in the mid-eighth century BCE was in the later Vedic period, the world evoked by the Brahmanas and the earliest Upanishads. The Rigveda had already been composed and orally transmitted; now the four-Veda canon was being systematised in the schools of ritual specialists on the Gangetic plain. Political life was organised around the sixteen mahajanapadas or 'great realms' that would coalesce over the following centuries into kingdoms such as Kosala, Magadha, Kuru, and Panchala, alongside oligarchic republics like the Vrijji. Iron technology, which had spread across the Ganges plain during the previous two centuries, was transforming agriculture and warfare, and Painted Grey Ware pottery marks the material culture of the northern plain. Around this time, according to Jain tradition, Parshvanatha, the twenty-third Tirthankara, was active and would die about 777 BCE; the great sramana movements that would produce the historical Mahavira and Buddha two and a half centuries later were beginning to stir. On the Central Asian steppe, mounted Iranian-speaking nomads — the ancestors of the Scythians, Sakas, and Cimmerians — were beginning the migrations that would soon disrupt Assyria, Urartu, and eventually Zhou China. The Indus valley cities had been abandoned for a millennium; villages of the Cemetery H culture and Painted Grey Ware farmers now occupied the plains between the Indus and the Ganges.

In the mid-eighth century BCE the Nile valley south of the First Cataract was under the rising power of the Kushite kingdom of Napata, whose kings ruled from the shadow of the sacred mountain Jebel Barkal and worshipped an Egyptianised form of the god Amun. The pivotal figure of the generation was Kashta (r. c. 760s BCE), who extended Napatan authority as far north as Thebes in Upper Egypt and had his daughter Amenirdis I installed as the God's Wife of Amun, a religious office of enormous political weight. His successor Piye would within decades sweep north through Middle Egypt and unify the country as the Twenty-Fifth Dynasty. Elsewhere along the African rim, Phoenician mariners from Tyre had recently founded, or were on the verge of founding, Carthage on the Tunisian coast (traditional date 814 BCE), which would soon become the great western Mediterranean power. In the Ethiopian highlands, D'mt or a closely related polity was beginning the settled agricultural life that would flower into the kingdom of Aksum a millennium later. Across the rest of sub-Saharan Africa the archaeological record for this specific year is thin, but the wider era saw the diffusion of iron metallurgy from the Nok culture of central Nigeria — active from roughly 1500 BCE with terracotta figurines and iron smelting — and the ongoing early phases of Bantu-speaking farming communities' expansion southward and eastward from the Cameroonian grasslands.

In Mesoamerica in the eighth century BCE the Olmec civilization of the Gulf Coast lowlands was in its Late Olmec phase, having already built the ceremonial centre of San Lorenzo (which peaked around 1200-900 BCE) and then the great mound-and-plaza complex of La Venta in modern Tabasco, occupied from roughly 900 to 400 BCE, with its colossal basalt heads, jade offerings, and Complex A cache. The Olmec style — jaguar imagery, hollow babies, an incipient calendar and glyphic notation — was radiating across highland Oaxaca, where the Zapotec ceremonial hilltop of Monte Albán would be founded around 500 BCE, and into the Valley of Mexico and the Maya lowlands, where the first modest ceremonial platforms were being raised. In the central Andes, the Chavín culture, centered on the ceremonial complex of Chavín de Huántar in the Peruvian highlands, was entering its long horizon of pan-Andean religious influence, its Lanzón and Raimondi Stela stone monuments defining a shared iconographic language from the coast to the sierra. In North America, Late Archaic and Early Woodland peoples were building modest mound and burial complexes in the Ohio and Mississippi valleys; the Poverty Point earthworks in Louisiana had been raised in earlier centuries and were still occupied. None of these societies had any contact with the eastern hemisphere; their trajectories were entirely their own.

Around the mid-first-millennium BCE, the Pacific was still being settled by seafaring peoples in the greatest maritime expansion in human history to that point. The Lapita cultural complex, defined by its distinctive dentate-stamped pottery, had already spread eastward from the Bismarck Archipelago through the Solomons, Vanuatu, and New Caledonia and reached Fiji, Tonga, and Samoa by around 900-800 BCE. In these western Polynesian archipelagos, Lapita settlers were laying down the linguistic, agricultural, and social foundations from which the later Polynesian expansion into eastern Polynesia — Tahiti, the Marquesas, Hawaii, Rapa Nui, and Aotearoa — would launch, in most cases not for another one to two thousand years. The Australian continent had been inhabited for tens of thousands of years by hundreds of distinct Aboriginal nations speaking hundreds of languages, sustained by intricate kin-based land tenure and firestick farming; the archaeological record of specific communities in this precise year is dispersed but the broader pattern of continent-wide occupation, songline networks, and long-distance exchange in ochre, stone, and shell was long established. In New Guinea, Papuan-speaking farmers in the highland valleys had by this time practised agriculture (taro, banana, sugarcane) at Kuk Swamp and elsewhere for perhaps seven thousand years.
